posts.name: Rick Joyer
posts.cooked:
Great pics, and fun idea for a thread, Buzz. I’m almost a little embarrassed to post these after seeing how nice your cellar is, but here goes…

We have an unfinished “crawl space” below our house, which is built on a steep hillside, and I converted a portion of it into an active cellar. It was definitely more of a “low-budget” high elbow-grease project that I undertook, but it was a lot of fun at the same time. The space used to be all dirt and exposed insulation, and looked like this (you can see the concrete footings I poured to frame my walls off of):

Digging to get things level sucked…I’m 6 feet tall, and the ceilings are about 5’6", so all the manual labor was done hunched over. That said, after a lot of sweat equity, beers and help from a few friends, it ended up morphing into a space that looked like this:

This was where things accelerated and got fun…as, at that point, I got the granite countertop installed, stuffed the thing to the gills with my wines and various wine-related items, and called it a finished product:

This gives you a view back to the entry door:

It is definitely more about function than show, what with decomposed granite floors and all, but it’s been a great (fun) learning experience and I’ve really enjoyed having a cellar at home.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Wow, Rick, that’s fabulous! I would kill to be able to do that, but live on the flats. What’s the natural temperature like in there, how much cooling do you need?

posts.name: Rick Joyer
posts.cooked:
Thanks fellas.

Alan - it gets pretty hot out here in the foothills during the summer time, but we have a nice exposure that protects us from the late afternoon sun. That said, temps below the house can creep into the low 70’s when we get a heat-spell, but the cellar keeps its temp quite well, especially considering it has an exposed DG floor. I let it go passive in the winter and just plugged the AC unit in the other day, which will run for the next 6 months or so.

posts.name: Chris Barnes
posts.cooked: My turn. This is what my wife and I just constructed while renovating our basement. There are a few more pieces of trim to put up, but that is scheduled for this weekend (which means it will happen next month). The back wall is a below grade cement block and so far has maintained a 60 degree environment with +/- 0.5 degree fluctuation. All other walls are insulated and it is passive for now. I bought the rough cut wood from a local sawyer and did all of the work myself. It was a very fun project. The bins are not ideal when you want to get something at the bottom, but function as a bit of cellar defender to. We wanted something fun to look at in our living space, and this fits the bill.

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
After years of passive cellars, we bit the bullet a few years ago. It was worth it. It is so much more fun to be able to look in CT, go downstairs, and pull that magnum of 2007 Saxum Bone Rock without having to risk sciatica from moving boxes hither and yon.

Designed for utility. I’ll bring the bottle upstairs to actually drink it. Through the door. Double deep racking on the sides. NOTE - despite concerns expressed by some, the Bouchard and Faiveley on the left with the Scarecrow and Schrader at the back do not result in spontaneous combustion just from being in the same room together.

View through the door of the right side wall - 24 columns - holds 948 bottles on that side.

posts.name: Chris Barnes
posts.cooked:
JoeD:

Chris - looks great! Can you provide some more detail on the glass material, door, and rough costs for said material?

Wood = 6/4 Poplar Approx $300
Floor = bamboo from Lumber Liquidators approx $100
Door = $500 custom order exterior 24 inch wide (with minimal reveal). I can get more info if you’d like.
Glass wall = $1000 I found a local guy who manufactured these insulated pieces for me
Lights = $100 I am using led

I think that is about it. I saved a ton by doing it all by myself (except having the glass delivered). It was well worth it though!

We built our cellar last fall. It’s utilitarian; it occupies the space under the stairs. No “display” row, fancy lighting, or cool wooden boxes in there. But the entrance is from the finished portion of our basement, so we tried to keep it from being a complete eye-sore. The empty gap in the racking is the space under the upper portion of the staircase. Eventually we plan to put a half-height double deep rack there with a table on top. Right now, there’s exposed ducting back in there.

It currently holds 993 bottles. Eventually, we’ll add two more rows on top (up to the ceiling), and with the double deep table, we should get close to 1300 bottles.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
I recently built my 3rd and hopefully last cellar. I outgrew the other 2, so I bit the bullet and consolidated everything into one. The things I wanted to have after learning from my previous cellars were:

  1. Storage for all of my bottles that I did not have the original packaging for, so I went with the 3 deep Vintage View metal racks. I also wanted to see the labels, not the capsules or the punts;
  2. It is a working cellar, so I wanted it to be bright - hence the light colored floors (reclaimed hickory) and LED ceiling and backlights. Music was also a must so 4 speakers;
  3. I wanted a place where I could unpack and inspect bottles and taste the wines. I was able to get a hickory slab from the same place I got the flooring and had a guy who I have used for tables before build the legs and and table base for me. All I had to do was put a few coats of poly on it. Oh and get it into my basement, that thing was freaking heavy.
  4. Safety and security - phone, video cameras with DVR, security system, online thermostat (Nest), fire and smoke detector (Nest Protect).
  5. There is only one problem - I am running out of space.

Chris - Great cellar. I am going to go out on a limb here and say that you are probably a fan of CDP. I like the OWC storage. I have my OWC’s stacked on top of each other and it always seems I am looking for a bottle from the OWC on the bottom. I need to look at making some built-ins. Any chance you have a picture of the OWC room with just the shelves before you stocked it?

Thanks for all the kind words!

I’m indeed a huge fan of CdP. How did you guess?

I’ll be there again this weekend for the Printemps du CdP; a great wine event in the center of the village.

I recognize the accessibility problem of wines in cases; I had that ‘problem’ as well in my previous cellar where I had a lot of cases stacked 8 to 10 cases high. The shelves work perfectly because every case can be accessed separately. Unfortunately I don’t have a picture of the empty shelves. They are however pretty easy to construct (especially when you compare it to the effort required for the construction of the rest of the cellar ).

The shelves and supports are made of around 35cm deep laminated wood. Vertically, the supports run from the bottom to the top (made from one piece). The horizontal shelves are pieces of laminated wood that are about 35cm wide (just an inch wider than the cases). These shelves are screwed between the vertical supports on each side (two screws on each side). Each subsequent vertical row of shelves starts 10 cm higher (or lower) than the previous vertical row. You can get the wood cut to the right dimensions at every DIY shop. It is easily fitted together if you put it flat of the floor (on the back of front of the ultimate shelves) and use two pieces of wood which you can use to place the shelves at the correct distance from each other (about 20 cm high).

Kindest regards,

Chris

Hello again, all. Regarding the several queries:

We installed this in an end room that was the workshop for a former carriage house we were renovating, so had a great weight-bearing concrete slab floor in place to handle the concentrated lode. The glass is actually tinted, but the tint disappears in photographs when the interior LED lights are on. My electric bills tell me it’s pretty efficient, especially once that mass of liquid and glass hit the targeted temperature. I know of several other glassed in-type cellars and am told of similarly successful results. We were able to use a split cooling system, built just against the building’s outside wall, with simple cut-throughs for intake/outtake purposes (then encased that cooling system for aesthetic purposes). Definitely let me know when any of you are in the Central Coast area and want to talk a look/help with inventory reduction – we enjoyed a dozen or so Berserkers and showcased a few producers during the last Berserkerfest up here, once the scavenger hunt filled up!

–JG

P. Willenberg:

I’m curious why some people store their bottles with the capsule facing out. I’m not great at reading upside down, so I’ve always done capsule in, punt out.

For me:

  1. Capsules are easier to identify which bottle it is. Punt is tougher.
  2. I have tags hanging on the neck to make each wine easier to identify vintage etc.
  3. When they’re loaded in the racks, it makes the racks lean back towards the wall. More secure. God forbid the rack pulls the screws out of the wall to fall over, it goes back into the wall rather than face-planting forward. Less of an issue.

Rick Joyer:

Great pics, and fun idea for a thread, Buzz. I’m almost a little embarrassed to post these after seeing how nice your cellar is, but here goes…

We have an unfinished “crawl space” below our house, which is built on a steep hillside, and I converted a portion of it into an active cellar. It was definitely more of a “low-budget” high elbow-grease project that I undertook, but it was a lot of fun at the same time. The space used to be all dirt and exposed insulation, and looked like this (you can see the concrete footings I poured to frame my walls off of):

Digging to get things level sucked…I’m 6 feet tall, and the ceilings are about 5’6", so all the manual labor was done hunched over. That said, after a lot of sweat equity, beers and help from a few friends, it ended up morphing into a space that looked like this:

This was where things accelerated and got fun…as, at that point, I got the granite countertop installed, stuffed the thing to the gills with my wines and various wine-related items, and called it a finished product:

This gives you a view back to the entry door:

It is definitely more about function than show, what with decomposed granite floors and all, but it’s been a great (fun) learning experience and I’ve really enjoyed having a cellar at home.

Rick - I think I spot a Rivers Marie Pinot on the shelf! I’m just getting started on mine, so these photos have given me some ideas.

In regards to dust almost none. My thinking is as follows:
Most dust in your home is made by us (70-80% human dead skin)
Wine cellar is cold.
Generally I walk in, close door behind me do what I ever I needed to do in cellar quickly and then exit to get out of the cold.

Don’t spend much time (under an hour weekly if not monthly) in there for enough skin to fall off and make dust.
I unpack most if not all my wines outside of the cellar and if need be dust them off. Nothing dirty goes in the cellar (aside from my mind of course), Been running cellar for 7 years, cleaned, swept and vacuumed it once thus far. June 2011 after breaking a bottle of wine on the floor (71 pertus in case anyone was wondering).

Since my cellar is built in basement with a lot of earth on two sides and underneath I wanted to maximize the passive cooling of the cellar and felt certain flooring could decrease it. I have since thought about adding a rubber mat on the walkway of the cellar to help cushion any future drops. I have also enacted the same rule about working in cellar as I have for cleaning wine glasses – ONLY SOBER!!! I try not to even enter my cellar when I am not sober I will often make poor decisions about what to drink next so I try to plan ahead days in advance and stand up wines and them remove from cellar the day of. posts.cooked:
P. Willenberg:
David Cohen:

Been running cellar for 7 years, cleaned, swept and vacuumed it once thus far. June 2011 after breaking a bottle of wine on the floor (71 pertus in case anyone was wondering).

Ouch!!

This brings up an interesting question. Does anyone have a floor covering? I’ve often wondered how much passive cooling I’d lose from the foundation if I covered the floor to guard against a broken bottle. My cellar is subterranean on the north side of the house in the old “root cellar” so it wouldn’t ever get above 65F if I didn’t have a cooling unit. The climatization just knocks it down to cellar temp in the summer.

Is the below-floor (soil) temperature below 55 (or your desired cellar temperature)? If not, then the floor covering will help keep the cellar cooler (or the cooling work less hard) because it’s like insulation and will slow the passage of heat from the floor into the cellar. If the soil temperature is below 55, then a floor covering will slow the transfer of heat into the ground but because you essentially have an unlimited source of cooling the only issue is if other heat sources to the room warm the room faster than the now-insulated slab can take away the heat.

All that said, floor covering is usually pretty low R-value because it’s thin - unless you plan on building a 6" subfloor with loads of insulation.

I know that full cases of Romanée Conti with their original circling have a higher value than the same bottles being outside of their cases. The difference is significant.

Despite that, I have decided to open all the cases in my cellar and to store only individual bottles.
The idea is that if burglars come in my cellar, the time to steal 12 bottles individually is probably 20 times bigger than to take one full cases.

After having seen above cellars with many full cases, I give this advice, to follow or not to follow depending on your appreciation of risks.

Mine is still ticking… like a Timex watch.

It has been almost 25 Years.
The Uniform Commercial Code, as it relates to secured financial transactions, defines a fixture as movable personal property that have become so related to real property that an interest in them arises under real property law. Think of your heating or cooling system for the non-lawyers here.

My Vinoteque is a fixture. When it arrived, despite my best measurements, it would not turn in the limited space in the stairwell to my basement. The movers un-crated the cooler and left it in my garage with a plan to pick it up and return it if I could not figure out what to do.
I ended up removing the landing to the stairs leading to my basement and lowered the unit down, and had the landing and some steps rebuilt.
It is now a fixture. Too expensive to remove. The buyer of my house has the option of keeping the unit. If they do not want it, a sawzall, hammer and some wrenches will remove it. I’ll use the racking and give away the compressor.

Marshall - If that happens, I would be happy to give you the compressor. Except, I don’t think the value of a 25 year old unit is worth the shipping costs East Coast to West Coast.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Some of the greatest wines I have ever had come from an old friend of a merchant I deal with in England. He fought in the Battle of Britain, and after the War, became an engineer.
After a dozen stellar bottles, I was in England and asked the merchant about him. He was ailing, but was happy to see visitors, so after lunch, we went over. When we got to the basement, there were a few bottles stuck in the corner, generic stuff not in the last bit interesting. He motioned us to the side and pressed a hidden button. A large section of the floor began to move, a little creaky that gradually revealed a set of stairs.

Down we went. The smell of an old great musty cellar hit me. Rows of old Bordeaux, from the forties onwards, a few mid range Burgundies, and a few Ports. But it was the Bordeaux that drew me; that day I bought a few, some Lafleur Pétrus 1947 and 1955, Lanessan 1961 and a single magnum of Trotanoy 1959.

I have seen a lot of cellars, but none as interesting, or with contents that wonderful. He was a man who knew and understood his wines, and an early lover of Pomerol long before it became fashionable. posts.name: Mike Reff
posts.cooked:
Some of the greatest wines I have ever had come from an old friend of a merchant I deal with in England. He fought in the Battle of Britain, and after the War, became an engineer.
After a dozen stellar bottles, I was in England and asked the merchant about him. He was ailing, but was happy to see visitors, so after lunch, we went over. When we got to the basement, there were a few bottles stuck in the corner, generic stuff not in the last bit interesting. He motioned us to the side and pressed a hidden button. A large section of the floor began to move, a little creaky that gradually revealed a set of stairs.

Down we went. The smell of an old great musty cellar hit me. Rows of old Bordeaux, from the forties onwards, a few mid range Burgundies, and a few Ports. But it was the Bordeaux that drew me; that day I bought a few, some Lafleur Pétrus 1947 and 1955, Lanessan 1961 and a single magnum of Trotanoy 1959.

I have seen a lot of cellars, but none as interesting, or with contents that wonderful. He was a man who knew and understood his wines, and an early lover of Pomerol long before it became fashionable. I still kick myself that I didn’t buy the few bottles of Pétrus, which were just outside my budget.

posts.name: Greg Piatig0rsk1
posts.cooked:
Some nice cellars.

Best I have ever seen is in Blackhawk (SF East Bay), a rather large house was half demolished, cellar dug in, and a nine+ car garage plus utility room/etc re-built on top. For 22,000 bottles, whites and reds sections separate and with separate cooling units, as well as another unit for kitchen/tasting room area. Full kitchen, dumbwaiter to haul wine up and down, tasting room/area probably 25 by 25 feet, massive centuries old table he found some place. Kitchen and tasting room can be warmed up.cooled down (and separated from actual storage areas by thick glass walls) in seconds, literally.Funny enough, that 22,000 bottles capacity was already seemingly reached and tested when I saw the place (as we unloaded wine to empty out wine shop back room, 2 Gorilla racks’ worth IIRC), with many wines sitting on the floor in cases/boxes with no room on shelves. Built by one of the most unassuming guys you’d ever meet, and one of the most generous when it came to sharing wine. I took a tour of the shelves, pretty much who’s who of wine world, Old and New, vintages old and new, anything one would ever want to taste.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
I am very impressed by Casey Shupe’s cellar for one reason: He built it himself!?!!! Don’t know what he does for a day job, but this is beautiful.

I have zero objection to those who want their cellars to be beautiful and elegant, and especially admire those who have the skill and taste to do what Mr. Shupe did. My basement makes me incredibly happy because I can go down there any evening and come up with something I want to drink, knowing that it has been bought in good condition and stored under ideal conditions. The bottles on the rack are all for drinking in the next few years. Temp on the slab is ~35 - 60 over the course of the year. Things age a little more slowly I would think than at a steady 55. Humidity is excellent as Maine is damp and the cellar is the opposite of airtight.

Dan Kravitz

Dan,
You’ve motivated me to post a picture of my own very modest wine storage area. It is in a separate “room” in my unfinished cellar (aka basement). My New England house foundation is constructed of large granite blocks with mortared joints - a real cellar. House is from the 1920’s and this was quite common at the time. I would guess the passive temperature range is similar to yours maybe 40’s to 60 max. I built the wine storage more than 30 years ago to get started putting some wines down and accumulating wines for special occasions. One of my earliest purchases was a case of 1980 Rieussec half bottles and 1982 Du Tertre for the birth years of my daughter Sarah and son Peter. To these, we added mostly single bottles of various wines for special occasions over many years. In 1997 while my wife and I were taking our first trip leaving the kids alone, Sarah and her high school cohorts got into the cellar wine storage and wiped it out. Yeah, locks are a very good idea.
Cheers,
-Jim

the floor is plywood panels covered in corks from all the bottles of wine we have ever opened (so I can take the panels with us when we buy a new house).

I really like the flooring idea. How did you make the flooring panels (or how did you attach the corks)?

Corks were attached with hot glue, one at a time, by my wife. I sorted the corks into the various producers and she would get them arranged and glued on. The racking is sitting on top of plywood as well, so when I put the floor panels in, I used a strip of wood and pin nailed it to the plywood sticking out from under the racking and the panel with the corks to hold it down.

Those of you who have fully framed and finished basement cellars, how much did they cost to construct? If you didn’t need to do any framing , just insulation, drywall, and racking (ie. turning an already existing basement room into a wine cellar), what do you suppose that might run if we weren’t looking to get too fancy with flooring/fixtures/etc?

I have an unfinished basement, with part of it framed and dry walled off for storage. I recently converted it myself into a wine cellar. Involved putting in insulation and sealing it up and putting a wine cooler unit into the wall. Also have 3 wine racks, with room for more as I build my collection. The insulation and sealing materials were ~1k, the cooling unit was 1.5k, and each wine rack that holds ~100 bottles is $500. WiFi temperature monitor is around $150.

I didn’t drywall over the pink board insulation, just painted it white. It’s definitely not a room to show off, but it serves its purpose and I could probably fit 1k bottles in there if I needed to. Holds a steady 55f quite nicely.

We wanted to be able to entertain in our underground cellar, but wanted to retain an old world look. As such, we did not want to have our wine behind glass. A great solution was, when building the cellar, to put heating coils under the stone floor, but only in the central area where the table is located. The wine is behind the arches (where the floor is not heated). The bottle temperature does not change while the guests are comfortably warmed at the table.

Colin

posts.name: Charles Modica
posts.cooked:
Might as well post mine.

New house build, 5’ x 9’ with 10’ ceiling. Goal was functional storage, but my wife (and builder) convinced me to add the stone on side/back. Note the 9000 BTU mini split – works perfectly (running since Feb 2020). Just changed the thermistor to “remap” temp setting – it’s set to 71 degrees but this cools to 55 degrees measured at the bottle. The white “object” below the mini split is a mini pump that can pump condensate back up thru PVC in the wall connected to a nearby bathroom sink drain, purposely set at that level to observe the status led - it rarely pumps due to the A/C cycle times.

I did end up adding a small dehumidifier kept on the floor that keeps the humidity at my targeted 55% as I was talked into “you don’t need a vapor barrier in dry San Diego county”. I had specified a vapor barrier but idiot insulation contractor installed it on the cellar side – so there was this long conversation about proper side and in the end I caved and allowed them to just not put one up. February and March 2020 humidity was fine (I was monitoring), and then climbed come April. For now I empty the dehumidifier reservoir every 2 days to 7 days depending on season. My temp/humidity monitor will send me an alert if the humidity climbs to 65%, which only happens when I’m slow to empty the reservoir and the dehumidifier doesn’t cycle.

Racking for 1002 Turley size bottles and 22 magnums, Wine Racks of America, Grand Mahogany. Racks to the right side are double deep racks. Single deep one column magnum rack all the way to right (just to the right of the door as you enter). I built the ladder myself using redwood 2x4’s, and the rolling hardware is using barn door hardware.

I think there’s around 700 bottles right now, but I need to redo my Cellar Tracker inventory. The wine was in offsite conditioned storage for six months between old house sold and new house construction finished, and I just haven’t gotten to going through all the wines and getting the inventory back in order – which is not good because that means I haven’t been looking at drink by dates since July 2019 when wines went to offsite locker storage. The past year has been a more or less random pull out / keep looking in terms of getting a bottle to go with dinner (wine every night).

I am finally able to contribute to this thread. We moved 7 years ago, and I moved out of my makeshift (but quite effective) home-built cellar and shoved everything in boxes and stored at an offsite. What a relief is is to finally have all my wine in one place again!

The room we chose was a single car garage, since converted to a daylight basement, and now converted to a wine cellar. The room is below grade on the long wine wall. Walls and ceiling are R21 closed cell, and the interior wall has extra blow-in cellulose insulation (noise reduction from the pipes in the wall). Pano shot to start with:

The cooling unit is a WhiskerKool ceiling mount 8000 split system. It was originally intended to go between the joists in the ceiling, but that became untenable due to all the “stuff” up there, and that we would have had to cut a joist and install additional supporting joists ($$$$), so our contractor built that soffit for the unit - not ideal, but it works.

All the individual bottle racking is double deep. Diamond bulk racks were originally going to be double deep until we realized what a pain it would be to get to the back bottles.


we have an 8’ redwood live-edge slab we need to finish, which will become a table for the center of the room.

D441166D-B480-467E-AE0F-FEF1F98B6D17.jpegI ran out of rack slots so I bought two of these display racks. 54 more spots for wine!

Bruce, nice cellar and display racks. I am in a similiar situtation, ran out of room in the cellar and am looking to add some display racks like yours. How did you mount them? Are they mounted on a stud or did you reinforce the wall and mount?

Each of the display racks are two parallel mounting brackets. The outer most bracket of the racks are screwed into the studs. The Inner brackets are installed using heavy duty drywall anchors. The anchors are supposed to each hold 50 lbs. I am more worried about earthquake than I am about the racks falling off the wall.

I have these display racks as well. It looks like the inner columns should be slightly further away from the outer columns, and ideally a touch higher so that it holds the neck of the bottle and the bottle is perfectly horizontal instead of tipping downwards. It looks weird when first installed because the inner column seems “higher” than the other, but it’s designed that way to catch the neck. Then I bought the rubber holders that the same company sells to hold the wine bottle in place for earthquakes. They’re very secure now.

posts.cooked:
Today, I replaced my dead monster Wine Guardian, which had worked flawlessly but with a lot of noise with a split system. After 14 years it stopped abruptly. I went through the options, and eventually chose the split. We took the decision on Tuesday, and it was installed today.


It was put in by a friend, Chris Kravitz, who did it in less than a day, and carted off the Guardian, hopefully to refurbish, and annoy others for years to come.

We used to be hear the Guardian from the second floor, this is so quiet, and although it has been less than a day, it seems to be working beautifully. I wish I had done it years ago.

If you are in the tri state area, I would heartily recommend Chris. Although he is a friend, he is also incredibly good, and one of the reasons is that he and his partner do not hire subs and do all the work themselves. It makes a huge difference, something I have come to know after a bathroom project was delayed and reworked thanks to cowboy subs.

posts.name: Christopher Sprague
posts.cooked:
Might as well share a pic of mine (passive, in Southern Maine), which was built about a year and a half ago based on principles in the book, How And Why To Build A Wine Cellar. Exterior walls insulated to R43, basement facing walls to R41, ceiling to R-51. I actually built the door plans from the book, which in my case is R-39. Annual temperature fluctuation is a slow and leisurely 54.7 - 65.1 (at least this year, last year was similar).

All of the racking is likewise DIY, and can currently accommodate 776 bottles. The Whisky (left) and Brandies (right) are only there for now because, well, why not? They add to the thermal mass of the room so help regulate temperature fluctuations, and it’s a good a place as any to store them. If I were to remove them, I could build additional racking to get the room’s wine storage capacity up to about 1100 bottles, at which point it would be maxed out. Current inventory actually stands at just under 600.

posts.name: Ken Leiter
posts.cooked:
I got around to taking some shoddy photos of my just completed cellar, which I DIY’d in the spring and early summer. This forum was an invaluable resource when planning and building the cellar. I wouldn’t be surprised if I read every single thread devoted to cellar building on the forum! Special thanks to @AndrewH whose posts I found extra helpful.

My goal was to maximize useful storage in a small space (6.5’ x 6’) in my basement, but I still wanted the convenience of individual racking. The racking on the left is double deep the entire length of the room and the racking on the right is double deep towards the back, with single depth magnum/champagne storage towards the front. I was concerned about the narrowness of the aisle between the double deep racks at 28.5”, but it is not bad at all. Racking is redwood from Wine Racks America. I purchased shelving so that I have additional bulk storage above the racks if I run out of room.

The floor is a floating cork floor with a dricore r+ subfloor underneath. Insulation is spray foam (I hired a company to spray). The cooler is a through the wall unit mounted directly above the door. If I had to do it over again, I’d probably not do it this way as I had to buy a custom height door, which was costly, but it is nice that the cooler is in an out of the way spot. The CellarPro 1800XTS cooler works well, but it is definitely louder than I had hoped. I upsized from the recommended 1800XT, and I probably should just have gone with the quieter XT model. I tried to be very careful with air-sealing the framing and the room is holding temperature pretty well. I am using SensorPush sensors to monitor temp and humidity and they work really well.

Overall, I am happy with the result. I spent a little bit more than I hoped originally, but not too bad - racking and cooler are expensive. I probably should have given some more consideration to running a coolbot with a standard air conditioner.

posts.name: Ted Smith
posts.cooked:
Gotta say there are some beautiful cellars in this thread. Almost embarrassed to post mine, but I’m proud none the less. We’ve got an unconditioned crawl space a little under 4’. When we bought the house it was a mess. Lots of gaps to the outside, fiberglass, terrible ground moisture barrier. I sealed up everything to the outside, replaced fiberglass with foam board, encapsulated with 14 mil, and got a dehumidifier down there. Now I know the dehumidifier is probably not great, I set at 55, but it’s a balance between mold or perfect cellar. Even in MW Montana I’ve kept the cellar pretty temp pretty constant on a seasonal basis. Daily swings of about 3-5 degrees, much less for actual bottle temps. Seasonally it fluctuates a bit more with the coldest around 48 and warmest around 65. It ain’t perfect, but it’s pretty good.

I was hoping having it in the crawlspace would keep me out of it…seems I’m down there adding, drinking or organizing more than my back likes…hahah

Thanks for all the inspirations for our next cellar

ErlendFA:

What is your estimated bottle capacity?

That’s a very good question. I wish I had a definitive answer, but I’m still trying to decide on what kind of racking I’ll use.

The finished dimensions should be about 66”x63”. Since it will be a small cellar, my inclination is to use bins as much as possible in order to maximize bottle capacity. If I do double deep bins across the back, and single depth bins along the left (up to 6-feet high), I should be able to store around 1,000 bottles.

If I use standard wooden racking (like from Wine Racks of America) with double deep along the back and single depth on the left side, them capacity drops to about 650 bottles. Everyone says this is more convenient than bins, but I’m not sure I can justify the loss of capacity.

posts.name: Chris C a r y
posts.cooked:
ErlendFA:

Do tell about that floor!
How long did that take you and did you accumulate the corks yourself?

I didn’t time myself, but remember many hours on my knees/butt with a glue gun. I did it mainly on Christmas breaks and with mostly my own corks. A friend from LA did send me a box of ~100. My first ~half was done in one “event”, then 3-4 other events once I had built up enough corks of our own drinking to make it worth the effort. Overall I think it took me 4-5 years total to complete. Around 3,000 corks total for a floor ~4 x 8. My cellar shape is a trapezoid shape, long story, so skinnier at the far end. I switched to a tube adhesive after the halfway point because the melty craft glue I started with tended to pop off under foot after a while. Rather than re-do the whole thing I repaired a bunch of loose corks and added the rug, which works well, cushiony, non-sticky and warm to bare feet. The cork floor has saved several bottles mostly Syrah shapes when tumbling from my lower rectangular bins.

These are (obviously) homemade racking and those bins aren’t as secure w those bottles. The strait side ones never any issues. My racks are left side Rhone/Pinot bottles size, right side Bordeaux/Italian bottle shape. My back wall rack was intended to be for magnums, but I outgrew that with more Pinot and Zin, so that’s its current occupants.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
I have these in my cellar, from the “Redrack” modular collection. The answer to your question depends on which module you get.

The F07 is ideal for most Bordeaux and Riesling, and some other regions that use Bordeaux style bottles (Spain and some Italian, mostly). These fit in one-bottle rows, five across, with almost no extra space (sometimes you have to a little jiggling to get all 5 in there). Very space efficient without having to stack bottles in bins. Burgundy-shaped bottles and heavier/thicker/broad-shouldered Bordeaux-shaped bottles (this seems to include all CA Bdx-shaped bottles, even the ones that don’t seem heavier, as well as some Italians and other non-Bdx Bdx-shaped bottles) will also fit, but only four across, with some wasted space. Less space-efficient. A very few of the very largest bottles won’t fit at all or have to be jammed in a bit.

The F01, F02 and F04 individual-bottle racks are the same as above except they are 4-across (8 across in the 01, but 32" wide) no matter what, so less efficient.

I have some open bins racks, but I’m not sure if they are F09 or perhaps an older model. They will hold 8 bottles per bin without having to be too persnickity about Bdx vs Burg shape, but too many of the very largest 750s in the same bin will bring that down to 7. Since they are stacked they are a little more space efficient than those above.

The F08 Magnum/Champagne will hold three Bdx-shaped mags plus a 750 of Champagne per row, so very space-efficient with those. With Burg-shaped mags or just bubbly all the way across, there is some wasted space. I don’t have any mags (including bubbly) that won’t fit in these height-wise, it’s just a matter of how many you can get per row, width-wise.

All in all, I have found these to be very good for the price but obviously some space efficiency and/or beauty could be gained with a custom setup, at a cost. They would probably be more space efficient for folks other than those with very Bdx and/or Riesling-focused cellars if they were a little wider so that the F07 held 5 across of either Bdx or Burg shaped bottles, but then they wouldn’t be 16" wide which of course helps with securing them to the studs.

Just finished loading up my extremely utilitarian, but very space efficient cellar. I converted an old 30x80 closet. Vapor barrier and insulation on the existing walls. After running wire for the cooling unit, I built a false ceiling using 3” foam insulation. I am using a whispercool though the wall unit. I built the doors myself. The French door design is key: it means the entire interior footprint is shelving, not space for me to stand. The shelves are plywood, 24” deep. I had cardboard tubes made. Most are 3.5 inch with one section at 4 to handle champagne and pinot. Everything is double deep. One cool trick: I used steel pipe, connected to the shelf, to crest a vertical separator so that I can use the full height of the space for most of the Edison but still leave the required clearances for cooling. The third picture shows that detail.

All in it will hold almost exactly 900 bottles. Total cost excluding the cooling unit was $1200.

That depends on the space. I went as short as possible because the outermost columns on each side end up behind the stub wall that holds the door frame. It’s only about 10 inches from the end of the tube to that wall, making access difficult to impossible. Basically, the end of the wine bottle would be stuck inside the tube as the other end hits the wall. By going short, I hoped to be able to squeeze in. On one side it works. On the other side 18.5” is still too long. I’ll either cut those down or just use that column for the few 375s I have.

If you have the depth available, I would go with 20” for double deep. I’m not sure on single as mine is 100% double.

posts.cooked:
We’re in the process of building a house and wine cellar along with it… hoping someone can give me some advice!

I specified with my builder that the cellar is to be insulated with closed cell foam insulation. Our ceiling is going to need 16" of space for the ducting to go through the ceiling so I asked him how he was handling the insulation since I bet they don’t want to spray 16" of insulation. He said it’s standard to only spray 1" of closed cell insulation and fill the rest with fiberglass insulation, even in the walls. I told him I thought that was incorrect, that at least in the walls are entirely filled with closed cell. Am I crazy?

posts.name: Eric White
posts.cooked:
Rajesh_P_a_r_i_k_h:

He said it’s standard to only spray 1" of closed cell insulation and fill the rest with fiberglass insulation, even in the walls. I told him I thought that was incorrect, that at least in the walls are entirely filled with closed cell. Am I crazy?

you absolutely would NOT fill it with closed cell, as CJ points out, 2" is sufficient. However, do take his advise and fill the remaining space with normal fiberglass insulation - not for the insulative (did I just make up a word?) properties, but due to the NOISE! Closed cell hardens rigid, and ends up amplifying sound (such as drains).

posts.name: Charles Perry
posts.cooked:
My 10 year-old nephew is staying with us at the lake this weekend, so the only work I was able to squeeze in on the wine cellar is applying the vapor barrier over the floor. The shelf platforms I built last weekend were removed, and plastic sheeting was spread over the cement floor. Edges of the vapor barrier were sealed with acoustical caulk, and the plastic was reinforced with tape where the vapor barrier makes contact the shelf platforms. Then the platforms were reinstalled.

IMG_72134032×3024 3.76 MB

IMG_72152016×1512 1.35 MB

Speaking of vapor barriers, I wanted to share a few tips for the benefit of those who discover this thread in the future while planning their own wine cellar:

Standard vapor barrier material (if you’re not using spray foam) is 6 mil polyethylene sheets. This is great stuff, except that standard glues, caulks, and tapes won’t stick to polyethylene. (At least not for long.)

The two best materials I’ve found for “sticking” polyethylene vapor barriers are (1) 3M Construction Seaming Tape (which is designed for polyethylene vapor barriers) and (2) non-skinning acoustical sealant. You must make sure that the acoustical sealant is “non-skinning.” This basically means that it never hardens. (There are also acoustical sealants that do cure or harden, but they will peel right off of polyethylene once they cure. And this will cause your vapor barrier to leak.)

Use the seaming tape to join two sheets of plastic or to patch holes. Use the acoustical sealant to “glue” the vapor barrier to studs, the floor, or just about anything else. Pro tip: Run a healthy bead of sealant along every stud that your vapor barrier makes contact with, so that when you hang drywall, the screws or nails go through the sealant and seal the hole that was made.

Non-skinning acoustical sealant is without a doubt the goopiest, stickiest, messiest substance I have ever encountered in my life. It sticks to absolutely everything. And since it never hardens, you can’t just peel it off after a few hours. I suggest you buy a box of rubber gloves and use them whenever you’re working with it. And wear a hat. Trust me, you don’t want this stuff in your hair.

Finally, at some point you are going to get acoustical sealant somewhere you don’t want it. Today I got it all over the finished pine I’m using for walls. It won’t clean up with water, and it’s damn near impossible to scrape off—the stuff just smears. But what I learned today is a game changer. If you put a little cooking oil on a paper towel, and then scrub at the acoustical sealant, it will come right off. Just wipe off the excess oil with a dry paper towel to avoid staining the material beneath it. It really works like a charm. I wish I had known about this trick months ago!

posts.name: Simon Haidamous

Finally wrapped up my new cellar here in Houston. This was a former coat closet. We have a flat roof and moved HVAC and other items to maximize ceiling height. The cooling unit sits on top of the roof, which isn’t ideal given our heat.

Went double depth for the racks, should hold around 1,200 or so bottles. I finally bit the bullet and logged all of the wines in CT before loading. I’m very happy with the finished product. Ben at AABC built the system. I chose him because he’s obsessed with cooling systems and he controls the entire project vs subbing out certain parts.
